rulings.Case Title: ALICE BOTTINO et al VS. CALIFORNIA PACIFIC BANK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Specifically Enforce The Parties Settlement Agreement And To Turn Over Funds
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, July 8, 2019, Line 1, Plaintiffs and Cross-Defendants Alice Bottino, as trustee of the Alice Bottino Trust, and Echco Sales Company's Motion to Specifically Enforce the Parties' Settlement Agreement and to Turn Over Funds. Plaintiffs and Cross-Defendants Alice Bottino, as trustee of the Alice Bottino Trust, and Echco Sales Company's motion to specifically enforce the parties' settlement agreement and to turn over funds is granted. The Settlement Agreement and Release provides that the full amount of the funds in the Blocked Account, with interest, shall be released to the Trust if, among other things, Site Closure is not obtained by April 13, 2014, a deadline that was later extended by the Court to April 13, 2016. It is undisputed that Defendant did not obtain Site Closure by the court-ordered deadline of April 13, 2016, and has not sought a further extension in the intervening three-plus years. Defendant also admits that it has not provided Plaintiffs' counsel with the quarterly reports required by the Settlement Agreement. These breaches of Defendant's obligations under the Settlement Agreement support exercise of the Court's reserved jurisdiction under Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6 to enter judgment on the agreement and to order the return to Plaintiffs of the funds in the Blocked Account. Defendant's arguments that the Settlement Agreement may not be specifically enforced under Civil Code Sections 3390 and 3391 are unpersuasive. Defendants' proposal to file a belated motion for a further extension of the Site Closure deadline would not cure those breaches. In any event, it is unclear that a further extension could produce a final resolution of this dated case within any reasonable period of time, given Defendants' consultant's prediction that site closure is unlikely to be achieved in less than an additional five years. Plaintiffs' motion for an award of attorneys' fees is granted in the amount of $51,156.00, plus $250.90 in costs. (Metcalf Decl. Par. 9.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CACH, LLC VS. FRANCISCO RIVAS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Void Default And Default Judgment(Ccp 473(D)) And To Quash Service Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, July 8, 2019, Line 2, Defendant Francisco Varela Rivas's Motion to Vacate Default and Default Judgment and to Quash Service of Summons. Defendant Francisco Varela Rivas's motion to vacate default and default judgment and to quash service of summons is granted. Defendant's motion is brought under Code of Civil Procedure Section 473(d), not Section 473.5. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 473(d) ["The Court may, . . on motion of either party after notice to the other party, set aside any void judgment or order"].) It therefore is timely, as "[l]ack of personal jurisdiction renders a judgment (or default) void, and the default may be directly challenged at any time." (Strathvale Holdings v. E.B.H. (2005) 126 Cal.App.4th 1241, 1250; see also Manson, Iver & York v. Black (2009) 176 Cal.App.4th 36, 42 ["A judgment that is void on its face may be set aside at any time."].) A judgment is void on its face when the invalidity is apparent upon an inspection of the judgment-roll, which includes the proof of service. (Calvert v. Al Binali (2018) 29 Cal.App.5th 954, 960-961; Trackman v. Kenney (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 175, 181-183.) Here, the proof of service reflects that the process server purported to effect substituted service on Defendant by leaving copies of the summons and complaint with an unnamed "co occupant." However, substituted service on an individual is not valid unless the summons and complaint "cannot with reasonable diligence be personally delivered to the person to be served." (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 415.20(b).) "' Ordinarily, . . . two or three attempts at personal service at a proper place should fully satisfy the requirement of reasonable diligence and allow substituted service to be made.'" (Trackman, 187 Cal.App.4th at 185.) Here, there is no declaration of diligence attached to the proof of service reflecting that any efforts were made to attempt personal service on Mr. Varela Rivas, and the proof of service therefore is facially void, which renders the judgment void as well. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: STONEBRAE CLUB PARTNERS, LLC VS. ROBERT WOLLACK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Stonebraes First Cause Of Action, For Breach Of Contract, In The First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, July 8, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF STONEBRAE CLUB PARTNERS, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Stonebraes First Cause Of Action, For Breach Of Contract, In The First Amended Complaint. Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Stonebrae Club Partners, LLC dba Stonebrae Country Club's motion for summary adjudication of the first cause of action for breach of contract in its first amended complaint is denied because there are triable issues of fact as to whether Stonebrae performed under the contract and whether Defendant and Cross-Complainant Robert Wollack is entitled to an offset based on the claims asserted in his first amended cross-complaint. (See Filet Menu, Inc. v. C.C.L. & G., Inc. (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 852, 862 [a party fraudulently induced into a contract may "seek to rescind the Contract or to affirm it. If affirmed, they could either sue for damages or set up the fraud as a defense and seek to offset their damages against any amounts recovered by the [defrauding party] in its action on its complaint]). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STONEBRAE CLUB PARTNERS, LLC VS. ROBERT WOLLACK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Notice Of Motion & Motion For Summary Judgment Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication Of Wollack'S Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, July 8, 2019, Line 5, CROSS-DEFENDANT STONEBRAE CLUB PARTNERS, LLC's Motion For Summary Judgment Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication Of Wollack's Cross-Complaint. Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Stonebrae Club Partners, LLC dba Stonebrae Country Club's motion for summary judgment and its alternative motion for summary adjudication of the first amended cross-complaint are denied. Because there is a triable issue of fact as to the viability of Wollack's "private club" intentional and negligent misrepresentation claims, summary adjudication is also denied as to the remaining causes of action for breach of contract, common count, violation of Business and Professions Code Secs. 17200, 17500, and Penal code Sec. 496, which are dependent on the misrepresentation claims. **The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to all counsel.** Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT KOPF VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Responses To Discovery; Requests For Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Molly A. Gilardi; Declaration Of Salayha Ghoury
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 08, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC's Motion To Compel Responses To Discovery; Requests For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No opposition filed. Motion is granted and sanctions is ordered against Plaintiff Robert Kopf and Plaintiff's attorney Steven Jacobson, jointly and severally, in the amount of $1,125. Plaintiff Robert Kopf shall provide complete, verified responses without objections to Defendant Uber's Special Interrogatories, Set One, and remit payment of sanctions to Defendant Uber within five (5) court days after notice of entry of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JIAN ZHU MA ET AL VS. MIKI LI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs' Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Discovery Responses And Appearances For Deposition, Memorandum In Support; And Declaration Of Palomar Sanchez
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 08, 2019, Line 7, PLAINTIFF JIAN MA, JIN MIA, JIA MA's Motion To Compel Discovery Responses And Appearances For Deposition. (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: No opposition filed. The court first notes that Plaintiffs failed to lodge courtesy copies of moving papers as required by San Francisco Local Rule 2.7 and did not respond to the court's request for courtesy copy by email. The court also notes that the proof of service of moving papers is defective. The court is inclined to deny the motion unless a code-compliant proof of service is presented at or before the hearing. In the alternative, the court is willing to receive live testimony from Megan Pham as to the service of moving papers. If the court is satisfied that the moving papers were properly served pursuant to C.C.P. §§ 1005 and 1013 the court is inclined to grant the motion in part and deny the motion in part: Motion to compel appearance at depositions is denied for Plaintiffs' failure to act diligently since discovery cut-off was June 24, 2019, and trial is set for July 22, 2019. The court moreover notes that Defendants' responses to Plaintiffs first set of written discovery requests were due on 9/28/19 but Plaintiffs failed to diligently pursue Defendants' lack of responses until February 12, 2019 when Plaintiffs sent a meet and confer letter. Plaintiffs failed to bring motion(s) to compel for Defendants' failure to respond until June 2019, more than eight (8) months later. Plaintiffs' counsel knew as early as August 2018 that Defendants cannot be reached by telephone. Therefore, Plaintiffs' meet and confer efforts, i.e., the telephone call in January 2019 before the letter of February 12, 2019, is unreasonable. The court also notes that Defendants' depositions were scheduled for March 14, 2019, Defendants did not appear and yet Plaintiffs did not incur court reporter fees for their non-appearance, indicating that Plaintiffs anticipated Defendants' non-appearance. (end of 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DALE PALMER VS. RESTAURANT CHEETAH GROUP, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, July 08, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT RESTAURANT CHEETAH GROUP, INC.'s Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination. Pro Tem Judge William Lynn, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is denied for Defendant Cheetah Group's failure to comply with the meet and confer requirements of C.C.P. § 2032.250. The court notes that Defendant Cheetah Group did not meet and confer prior to the I.M.E. and proceeded with the I.M.E, that the I.M.E. was not suspended pursuant to C.C.P. § 2032.510, and that Defendant Cheetah Group did not move for a protective order pursuant to C.C.P. § 2032.510. Therefore, under these facts, Defendant Cheetah Group already had its one I.M.E. as provided by C.C.P. § 2032.220. To the extent that the motion was brought pursuant to C.C.P. § 2032.310, the motion is denied for defective notice of motion without prejudice to Defendant Cheetah Group's right to bring another properly noticed motion pursuant to C.C.P. § 2032.310. The court moreover notes that the parties' subsequent "meet and confer" does not appear to be a reasonable, good faith attempt at informal resolution of the issues. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to wlynn@rroyselaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, then the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing if the tentative ruling is contested. If the tentative is not contested and there is no hearing on the motion, then Defendant must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANDRES LOPEZ PINZON VS. RECOLOGY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, July 8, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANTS RECOLOGY, INC., GOLDEN GATE DISPOSAL & RECYCLING COMPANY, AND JOHN BORZONI'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Recology, Inc., Golden Gate Disposal & Recycling Company, and John Borzoni's demurrer to Plaintiff Andres Lopez Pinzon's first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled as to both causes of action for motor vehicle negligence and premises liability. "The existence of a duty of care toward an interest of another worthy of legal protection is the essential prerequisite to a negligence cause of action, determined as a matter of law by the court." (Software Design & Application, Ltd. v. Hoefer & Arnett, Inc. (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 472, 478.) While the Court's April 15, 2019 order concluded that garbage collection companies do not have a duty of care to warn individuals of the risks of secreting themselves in dumpsters, that order was limited to an analysis of the facts alleged in the initial complaint. The FAC adds new allegations that are sufficient to give rise to such a duty. (FAC Paras. 11-16, 24-28.) In light of the new facts alleged in the FAC, an analysis of the Rowland factors laid out in Cabral v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2011) 51 Cal.4th 764, 771 [citing Rowland v. Christian (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108] yields the conclusion that the injury to Plaintiff was foreseeable and that it would not be unreasonably burdensome to impose a duty on garbage collectors to rattle dumpsters before dumping the contents into their trucks. "The most important factor to consider in determining whether to create an exception to the general duty to exercise ordinary care articulated by section 1714 is whether the injury in question was foreseeable." (Kesner v. Superior Court (2016) 1 Cal.5th 1132, 1145.) The FAC pleads that Defendants were aware of prior instances of garbage collectors encountering persons inside of dumpsters, including one specific incident on July 22, 2014, which preceded the accident that gave rise to this case. (FAC Paras. 12-14.) The FAC also alleges that Recology and Golden Gate advise garbage truck drivers of the risks of homeless people seeking shelter in dumpsters and that they should "rattle the bins" before emptying them. (FAC Para. 15.) These allegations, combined with more specific allegations that homeless people enter dumpsters for shelter, recyclables, and food, are enough to conclude that the injury to Plaintiff was foreseeable. (FAC Para. 11.) While the facts alleged suggest only minimal moral blame, they also show that the costs and burdens on Defendants to rattle dumpsters or otherwise warn hidden individuals of the imminent dumping of the trash are minimal. (FAC Para. 16.) Therefore, as pled, the FAC presents facts that distinguish this case from other cases where societal burdens of imposing a duty were much greater. (See Cabral, 51 Cal.4th at 772 [citing cases in which courts declined to find a tort duty in light of the societal burdens that recognizing such a duty would create].) A duty on the part of drivers to rattle a dumpster is not unduly burdensome. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ANDRES LOPEZ PINZON VS. RECOLOGY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Monday, July 8, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT SUE MILLS, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Sue Mills, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Andres Lopez Pinzon's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained without leave to amend as to the second cause of action for premises liability. The potential risk of immersing oneself in a dumpster is obvious as a matter of law. (See Christoff v. Union Pacific Railroad Co. (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 118, 126 [holding that railroad tracks provide a self-evident warning to an adult of ordinary intelligence "that it is not safe to walk upon it, or near enough to it to be struck by a passing train"]; Huffman v. City of Poway (2000) 84 Cal.App.4th 975, 991 [holding that the obviousness of a condition is not a question of fact where "reasonable minds can come to only one conclusion"].) While Defendant may have a duty to take precautions to protect against risk of harm from an obviously unsafe condition, even if it does not have a duty to warn, it must be foreseeable that someone would encounter the condition. (See Osborn v. Mission Ready Mix (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 104, 121-22 ["[A]lthough the obviousness of a danger may obviate the duty to warn of its existence, if it is foreseeable that the danger may cause injury despite the fact that it is obvious (e.g., when necessity requires persons to encounter it), there may be a duty to remedy the danger, and the breach of that duty may in turn form the basis for liability, if the breach of duty was a proximate cause of any injury."] [emphasis in original].) The existence of such a duty turns on issues of foreseeability, the burden on the defendant, and the consequences to the community of imposing such a duty. (Id. at 121.) Finding a duty would not require Sue Mills to abandon trash service, but it would impose burdens by requiring businesses to obtain locks, make signs, or check dumpsters for people on trash day. Moreover, while the FAC alleges that the business is located in an impoverished neighborhood with homeless people who may have reasons to search in dumpsters for shelter, food, or other supplies, it does not allege that an accident such as this has occurred on these premises before. (FAC Par. 24.) The trash collection defendants are better situated to warn individuals in dumpsters before the contents are dumped into their trucks than businesses who merely house dumpsters on their properties. Finally, Plaintiff does not allege that he was foreseeably required to immerse himself in the dumpster, thus subjecting himself to the dangers imposed by the obviously dangerous condition. (See Jacobs v. Coldwell (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 438, 447 [no duty because there was no evidence that plaintiff was "foreseeably required to expose himself to the danger of falling into the empty pool"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Sue Mills, Inc. is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
